CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-19-2020 Continued To Aug-19-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-30-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PHYSICIAN'S SERVICE DBA BLUE SHIELD'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendant Blue Shield's demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the sixth cause of action and overruled as to the first and second causes of action.

Defendant's demurrer as to the sixth cause of action for negligence, on the basis that such a claim cannot be stated against an insurer, is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. "[T]he California Supreme Court has identified the limited circumstances where the litigants' relationship, which has its basis in a contract, can give rise to a tort claim." (Benavides v. State Farm General Ins. Co. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 1241, 1251.) A claim of negligence does not lie against an insurer for merely denying coverage. (See Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 551-552 ["Tort damages have been permitted in contract cases where a breach of duty directly causes physical injury; for breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing in insurance contracts; for wrongful discharge in violation of fundamental public policy; or where the contract was fraudulently induced. In each of these cases, the duty that gives rise to tort liability is either completely independent of the contract or arises from conduct which is both intentional and intended to harm . . . The question thus remains: is the mere negligent breach of a contract sufficient? The answer is no."].) Plaintiffs concede that the first cause of action as pled is limited to claims handling and processing, but seek leave to amend, which is granted.

Defendant's demurrer as to the sixth cause of action on the basis of plaintiff's inability to state a bystander claim is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff Elizabeth Thomas concedes she cannot state a bystander claim, but seeks leave to amend, which is granted. Defendant may test the viability of Mrs. Thomas's direct-victim theory of liability, should she choose to assert it, by demurring to her amended cause of action.

Defendant's demurrer as to the first, second, and sixth causes of action on the basis of uncertainty is overruled. A "demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.) What actions Blue Shield is responsible for, as opposed to those taken by its mental health services administrator and its parent corporation, can be clarified during discovery. Further, this information is presumptively within Blue Shield's knowledge, rather than Plaintiffs'. (See Chen v. Berenjian (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 811, 822 ["A demurrer for uncertainty will almost certainly be overruled where the facts alleged in the complaint are presumptively within the knowledge of the demurring party or ascertainable by invoking discovery procedures."].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com (302/EPS)
